title: Document the ≥2-label proxy-domain constraint for wildcard TLS
what_xcind_learned: Scind ADR-0009's promise that an `auto`-mode wildcard cert `*.${domain}` makes TLS 'just work for every generated hostname' is false for single-label domains: `*.localhost` has a valid SAN but is REJECTED by RFC-6125-strict stacks (LibreSSL/macOS system curl, Apple Secure Transport/Safari, Go crypto/tls) because a wildcard is only honored when its base has ≥1 dot; wildcards also match exactly one label deep. OpenSSL/Chrome accept it (lenient), which masked the bug. Xcind hit this with its `localhost` default and moved the default to `localhost.scind.io`.
rationale: §2 directionality: this teaches the design was incomplete, not merely how Xcind built it. Single-label rejection is a property of TLS matchers, so it breaks ANY implementation (Go included) that provisions `*.singlelabel`. Scind's default `scind.test` happens to satisfy the constraint, which is exactly why the flaw stayed latent and unwritten in canon.
proposed_action: Amend docs/decisions/0009-flexible-tls-configuration.md (and note in docs/specs/proxy-infrastructure.md TLS section): add a constraint that any proxy domain used for wildcard TLS MUST contain ≥1 dot (≥2 labels) under a non-disabled TLS mode, because strict RFC-6125 stacks reject `*.singlelabel` even with a present SAN; wildcards match one label deep. Implementations SHOULD warn (not fail) on a single-label domain. Optionally port the HSTS-TLD caveat (`.io` not preloaded, unlike `.dev`/`.app`).

title: Project-name isolation must account for multiple working copies (git worktrees) of one repo
what_xcind_learned: Deriving the Compose project name and workspace-internal network from `{workspace}-{application}` silently assumes one working copy per project. Run from multiple linked git worktrees, every worktree derives the SAME project name and network, so `up` in one adopts/disrupts the other's containers and shares its network — collision, not isolation. Xcind added a single per-worktree token `XCIND_INSTANCE`, resolved once per run (explicit env/.override wins; `XCIND_INSTANCE_AUTO=0` opts out; else auto-detect the sanitized worktree-dir basename when `git rev-parse --absolute-git-dir` != `--git-common-dir`), folded into exactly two derived names (project name and `{workspace}-{instance}-internal`). The main worktree resolves to an EMPTY token, excluded from the cache SHA, so existing checkouts stay byte-identical.
rationale: §2/§2a Example B calibration case. The canon-neutrality test fails: you cannot write 'why Scind should not adopt a per-instance token' because multi-environment-on-one-host is Scind's core promise and worktree collision is a genuine hole in it. The collision occurs in any implementation of ADR-0001 as written, Go included — a design-assumption defect, not a Bash artifact.
proposed_action: Amend docs/decisions/0001-docker-compose-project-name-isolation.md to state {workspace}-{application} assumes one working copy per project and introduce a per-instance disambiguation token that EXTENDS (not replaces) the project name; empty token = current behavior (backward compatible). Amend docs/specs/naming-conventions.md: fold the instance token into the project-name and internal-network patterns ({workspace}-{instance}-internal when non-empty), extend the collision-warning section to name the multi-worktree case, and specify resolution order (explicit > opt-out > auto-detect-from-linked-worktree, keyed on stable worktree dir identity, not branch).

title: Assigned exports need three vars — Scind's in-network _HOST paired with a host-side _PORT is internally inconsistent
what_xcind_learned: Scind's service-discovery spec gives an assigned export two vars: `_HOST` = in-network alias (e.g. `shared-db-db`) and `_PORT` = the assigned HOST port. When the host port differs from the container port (normal after a conflict reassignment), the pair is self-contradictory: in-network the container listens on its container port, so `${HOST}:${PORT}` resolves wrong from inside a container, and the alias is unreachable from the host. Xcind found containers need BOTH the in-network pair (host + container port) and the host-published port, and split the schema into three: `_HOST` (in-network alias) + `_PORT` (container port) + `_HOST_PORT` (allocated host port).
rationale: §2 directionality: Xcind proved Scind's assigned-export env design incoherent by construction — an in-network `_HOST` cannot be combined with a host-side `_PORT`. Canon-neutrality test fails: there is no sentence explaining why Scind should keep an unusable host/port pairing. Self-flagged PROMOTE in ADR-0018 but stronger than a promotion — it's a defect fix. Language-independent.
proposed_action: Fix docs/specs/environment-variables.md assigned-export rules to three values: `SCIND_{APP}_{E}_HOST` = in-network alias, `SCIND_{APP}_{E}_PORT` = container port (container-to-container use), `SCIND_{APP}_{E}_HOST_PORT` = allocated host-published port (host.docker.internal / host access). Update the Variable Generation Rules table row and fix docs/specs/generated-manifest.md + docs/reference/configuration.md so host_port and container_port surface separately.

title: Service discovery must offer a host-view rendering, not only the container view
what_xcind_learned: Injected discovery vars are container-flavored, but developers also run host processes (mise/direnv, test runners, language servers, debuggers) that must reach the same services — on the host an assigned export lives at `127.0.0.1:<host_port>`, not the in-network `host:container_port` a container uses. So one committed expression (`DATABASE_URL=...@${.._HOST}:${.._PORT}/...`) cannot resolve correctly in both places; authors hardcoded two forms or hand-maintained a parallel host `.env`, reintroducing the drift env injection set out to kill. Xcind added an opt-in host-view env file (`XCIND_HOST_ENV_FILE`/`_MODE`) rendering the SAME discovery computation with host-flavored values, relying on two layering facts (dotenv loaders never override a real OS env var; Compose `environment:` beats `env_file:`) so one committed expression yields the container endpoint in a container and the host endpoint on the host.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's discovery promise ('reference other services without hardcoding, no drift') silently holds only inside containers. The host/container endpoint disagreement is intrinsic to Docker port publishing (true in Go too); the symmetry mechanism relies on language-agnostic layering facts. ADR-0020 self-flags PROMOTE. ALTERNATIVE READING captured: if Scind rules host-side access out of its runtime model this reduces to an opt-in convenience — but that scoping is a P6 reconciliation call, and defaulting to DIVERGENCE would violate burden-of-proof. Depends on adopting L-0003.
proposed_action: Add a 'Host-View Environment' section to docs/specs/environment-variables.md: an opt-in host-view env file whose assigned-export values use `127.0.0.1` + host port while proxied/apex hosts keep the routable hostname, sourced from the SAME computation as container injection so the two cannot drift; document the two precedence facts; make it opt-in with an explicit own/block write mode never inferred from the filename. If P6 rules host-side access out of scope, downgrade to a noted divergence.

title: Assigned-port artifacts are live-state-derived and must not be cached as a pure function of tracked config
what_xcind_learned: Xcind cached the assigned-port compose overlay alongside pure GENERATE overlays keyed only on a config/compose content SHA. Because assigned-port output actually depends on LIVE global state (the assigned-ports TSV registry + a port-availability probe) that is NOT in the cache key, the overlay silently diverged from reality whenever that hidden state changed (prune, manual edit, competing allocation) without a config change. Fix: an `XCIND_HOOKS_ALWAYS` class (assigned + discovery hooks) that stays in the ordering/marker/completeness accounting but is re-executed against live state on cache hit instead of replayed.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's design is silent/naïve in a way any implementation inherits. Scind's staleness detection watches source-file mtimes (workspace.yaml/application.yaml/state.yaml/compose) but NOT the global `~/.config/scind/state.yaml` assigned_ports, and port-types.md never says assigned host-port values derive from live global state. Another workspace's `scind port release/gc` mutates global state, the baked host_port goes stale, and no watched source mtime changed — the identical latent bug. The POC disproved the pure-function-of-tracked-files assumption; a design gap, not a Bash artifact.
proposed_action: In docs/specs/port-types.md, state that `assigned` port values resolve against live global port state and are not a pure function of application config. In docs/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md 'Staleness Detection', add the global assigned-ports state (or an assigned-port revalidation step) as a regeneration trigger, and define an 'always-regenerate' class of override content (analogous to XCIND_HOOKS_ALWAYS) for live-state-derived artifacts. Cross-link docs/specs/state-management.md.

title: Generated artifacts embedding assigned/live values must be written AFTER allocation, or direct consumers read stale state
what_xcind_learned: Xcind wrote its cache `config.json` (the introspection artifact external tools read directly) inside populate-cache, which ran BEFORE the assigned-port hook allocated/upserted the port. On a cache miss, direct readers saw missing/stale `assignedExports` until a later run self-healed. The CLI path was unaffected only because it re-resolved JSON after hooks. Fix split the JSON write into a post-hook step.
rationale: §2 directionality: Xcind proved a real ordering constraint Scind's spec never makes explicit. Scind's generated-manifest.md is sold as a direct-consumer artifact (dashboards, DNS updaters) that bakes host_port/apex/env values, and workspace-lifecycle.md 'Generation Logic' lists override (step 8) and manifest (step 10) writes but has NO explicit assigned-port allocation step and never states allocation must precede those writes. A Scind impl that materializes the manifest before allocating reproduces the stale-consumer bug.
proposed_action: In docs/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md 'Generation Logic', add an explicit assigned-port allocation/validation step and require it to precede the override (step 8) and manifest (step 10) writes. In docs/specs/generated-manifest.md, add a freshness contract: because the manifest is consumed directly, it must be written after allocation and reflect post-allocation host_port/env values.

title: Regeneration/staleness must catch partial output and generator-version changes, not only source mtime
what_xcind_learned: Xcind treated the mere existence of the generated dir as a cache hit and created it before all hooks ran, so a crash/failed hook left a partial dir replayed as complete; and a stable SHA from an older Xcind version masked newly-added default hooks after an upgrade. Fix made generation atomic (temp dir + rename), added a `.complete` marker plus per-hook completeness checks, and cleaned up partial state on failure. Separately, a failed `docker compose config` could write a truncated resolved config into the cache with no error propagation.
rationale: §2 directionality: two blind spots in Scind's mtime-only staleness model. (1) mtime cannot detect a truncated/partial override from a crashed generation — the file mtime is fresh but content is incomplete. (2) Upgrading the Scind binary to emit new labels/hooks touches no watched source mtime, so overrides silently survive stale after a schema change. A faithful Scind implementation carries both defects.
proposed_action: In docs/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md 'Staleness Detection', require generation to be atomic (temp + rename) with a completeness marker, treat partial/incomplete output as stale, and add a generator/schema version to the staleness inputs so a Scind upgrade forces regeneration. Note that a failed resolve step (`docker compose config` equivalent) must fail generation rather than persist a truncated artifact.

title: Specify the HTTP→HTTPS redirect mechanism and the redirect-middleware-on-every-service-block constraint
what_xcind_learned: Scind's proxy-infrastructure spec describes the `web` entrypoint as 'HTTP traffic (redirects to HTTPS)' with no mechanism. Xcind implemented `tls=require` by attaching Traefik's `redirectscheme` middleware to a redirect-only HTTP router, and discovered a non-obvious constraint: because Traefik's Docker provider only loads labels from RUNNING containers, the shared middleware DEFINITION must be emitted on EVERY service block of the overlay (not just a 'first' service), or the reference dangles whenever that one service is down. Repeated identical definitions are idempotent.
rationale: §2 directionality: canon under-specifies the redirect ('redirects to HTTPS' with no how); the Bash PoC surfaced both the concrete mechanism and an emergent Docker-provider constraint any label-based implementation (Go included) must satisfy — inherent to Traefik's label-scoping, not a Bash artifact.
proposed_action: In docs/specs/proxy-infrastructure.md (Entry Points / Dynamic Routing) and docs/specs/docker-labels.md, specify that HTTP→HTTPS redirect is realized via a `redirectscheme` middleware attached to the HTTP router, and add an implementation note that the middleware DEFINITION must be repeated on every rendered service block because Traefik's Docker provider reads labels only from running containers (idempotent repetition).

title: Traefik static config should emit websecure entrypoint + file provider + cert mounts only when TLS is enabled
what_xcind_learned: Scind's canonical traefik.yaml/traefik-compose.yaml ALWAYS declare the `websecure` (:443) entrypoint, the `file` provider watching `/etc/traefik/dynamic`, and bind-mount `./dynamic` + `./certs` — even though ADR-0009's `disabled` mode means no TLS termination at the proxy. Xcind proved these should be conditional: with `XCIND_PROXY_TLS_MODE=disabled` it omits the websecure entrypoint, file provider, `:443` mapping, and cert/dynamic mounts, avoiding a proxy that advertises HTTPS / watches a cert dir that will never be populated.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's static config templates are unconditional and inconsistent with its own `disabled` TLS mode; the PoC showed the config must be TLS-mode-conditional to be coherent. A design refinement of canon, not a Bash-specific detail.
proposed_action: In docs/specs/proxy-infrastructure.md and docs/specs/appendices/proxy-infrastructure/{traefik-config.yaml,traefik-compose.yaml}, annotate the websecure entrypoint, file provider, `:443` port, and `./certs`/`./dynamic` mounts as emitted only when `tls.mode != disabled` (mirroring how the dashboard is already conditional in Scind).

title: Proxy HTTP/HTTPS/dashboard ports should be configurable, not hardcoded 80/443/8080
what_xcind_learned: Scind hardcodes `80:80`, `443:443`, `8080:8080` in its Traefik compose. Xcind made host-side ports configurable via `XCIND_PROXY_HTTP_PORT` (default 80), `XCIND_PROXY_HTTPS_PORT` (default 443), `XCIND_PROXY_DASHBOARD_PORT`, so the shared proxy can run when 80/443 are already occupied (common on dev machines) without editing generated files.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's fixed ports are a real usability limitation the PoC hit and solved; exposing them as config is a canon gap, not a language artifact. Low weight — incremental ergonomics, not a correctness defect.
proposed_action: Add `proxy.http_port` / `proxy.https_port` (and reuse the existing `proxy.dashboard.port`) to the docs/specs/configuration-schemas.md proxy schema, and reference them in the traefik-compose appendix `ports:` block instead of literals.

title: Add a preferred-scheme `.url` label so consumers need no scheme-selection logic
what_xcind_learned: Alongside `.http.url`/`.https.url`, Xcind emits a single preferred-scheme `xcind.export.{name}.url` (and `xcind.apex.url`) giving consumers the canonical URL without scheme logic (HTTPS when an HTTPS router exists, else HTTP). Scind exposes only the per-protocol `.proxy.{proto}.url` pair.
rationale: §2 directionality: the difference flows Xcind→Scind. A preferred-scheme label is an additive ergonomic that removes scheme-selection logic from every consumer. (Xcind's flattening from `.proxy.http.url` to `.http.url` is cosmetic naming, NOT proposed as canon.)
proposed_action: In docs/specs/docker-labels.md, add a preferred-scheme `scind.export.{name}.proxy.url` / `scind.apex.proxy.url` label (HTTPS when an HTTPS router exists, else HTTP), additive to the existing per-protocol URL labels.

title: A single --json contract should back both label generation and introspection, and read-only introspection must be side-effect-free
what_xcind_learned: Xcind found per-app routing state (assigned ports, proxied URLs) had no readable contract — answering 'what port did db get?' meant docker inspect/jq gymnastics. It made `xcind-config --json` (`assignedExports`/`proxiedExports` maps) the single source of truth that BOTH the Traefik label generator and the introspection commands read via shared helpers, so labels and reported values cannot drift. It also learned read-only introspection must NOT request a generated compose config, because that triggers the proxy EXECUTE path (TLS cert provisioning, proxy startup) — inappropriate for a read and failing in constrained CI without mkcert/openssl.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind specs `scind app show`/`scind urls` outputs but defines no stable machine contract shared by label generation and read commands, nor a read-vs-execute isolation guarantee. Both the drift-prevention (one computed contract feeds labels + reports) and the read-must-not-provision insight are language-independent — a Go build hits the same 'introspection accidentally started the proxy' hazard.
proposed_action: In docs/reference/cli.md (or a new specs contract doc): (1) define a stable `--json` introspection contract (`assignedExports`/`proxiedExports`, keyed by export name) that is the single source both Traefik label generation and read/show commands consume; (2) state that read-only commands (show/urls/ports) must resolve from persisted/config state and must never trigger generation, cert provisioning, or proxy startup as a side effect.

title: Reporting/introspection surfaces should prefer the apex URL for the headlining export
what_xcind_learned: The app is reachable at the short apex URL (`{app}.{domain}`) — the URL users actually type — but Xcind's reporting only showed the longer per-export hostname; the apex lived only inside the GENERATE hook. Xcind (ADR-0017) propagated `apex_url`/`apex_host` into the JSON contract additively (preserving per-export `url`) so `urls`/`exports`/`status` prefer the apex for the first proxied export. Scind already bakes apex into its manifest and labels, but its CLI reference (`scind urls`) example surfaces only per-export URLs.
rationale: §2 directionality: this is about WHICH URL to display (reporting completeness), distinct from HOW the primary is chosen (see L-0018/L-0028). Scind already models apex in generated-manifest.md and docker-labels.md (CANON-CONFIRM on that side), but its CLI reporting reference never surfaces the apex, so a faithful Scind impl reproduces Xcind's original invisible-apex gap.
proposed_action: Update docs/reference/cli.md so `scind urls` and `scind app show` report the apex URL for the primary/headlining proxied export (keeping the per-export URL in the detailed view), and add `apex`/`apex_host` as additive fields in the JSON output contract, derived from the same source as the `scind.apex.*` labels.

title: First-class application-level env-file injection into all services (distinct from compose-interpolation env files)
what_xcind_learned: Docker Compose has two separate env-file scopes — a common pitfall: `--env-file`/`COMPOSE_ENV_FILES` drives `${VAR}` interpolation of the YAML only, while `env_file:` in a service injects vars into the running container. Xcind's original single `XCIND_ENV_FILES` conflated them (the name implied container availability but it only did interpolation). Xcind split them into `XCIND_COMPOSE_ENV_FILES` (interpolation) and `XCIND_APP_ENV_FILES` (a hook that injects `env_file:` into every service), so user env files are made available inside all containers uniformly.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind, also a Compose overlay wrapper, has no concept of user-supplied env-file injection into containers — its env story is only generated SCIND_* discovery vars plus hand-written `env_file:`. The compose-level-vs-container-level distinction is a real documented Compose pitfall any wrapper faces; Xcind proved a first-class mechanism (and the naming split disambiguating the two scopes) is needed. A design gap, not a Bash artifact.
proposed_action: Add an application-level env-file injection concept to Scind config (e.g. an `env_files:`/`app_env_files:` key on application.yaml or workspace overrides) that generates `env_file:` entries on every service in the generated override, distinct from any compose-interpolation env-file mechanism. Document the two-scope distinction explicitly in docs/reference/configuration.md. If Scind intends users to hand-author `env_file:` per compose service instead, record as a deliberate divergence — but the automatic all-services injection is the capability gap.

title: Re-running a config-writing command must preserve unrelated user-authored fields
what_xcind_learned: Re-running `xcind-workspace init` with a flag sourced only two known variables from the existing `.xcind.sh`, then rewrote the whole file, silently dropping any other hand-edited settings (hook arrays, extra XCIND_* vars). Fix switched to targeted key updates that preserve unrelated config; the same class was found for application init.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's workspace-lifecycle.md defines `workspace init` but is silent on re-run/update semantics for workspace.yaml, though it DOES give an explicit preservation guarantee for the overrides/ directory. Xcind proved a config-mutating command that regenerates rather than edits will drop unrelated user fields — a data-loss hazard the spec should foreclose. (The whole-file rewrite is a Bash artifact; the preservation principle is design-level.)
proposed_action: In docs/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md, state that re-running `workspace init` (or any command writing workspace.yaml/application.yaml) performs targeted field updates and preserves unrelated user-authored fields, extending the existing overrides/ preservation guarantee to the primary config files.

title: Validate resolved/inferred port values at generation time, not downstream in Traefik
what_xcind_learned: Xcind emitted whatever followed `:` in an export as the Traefik loadbalancer server.port with no validation, so `web:notaport` or a `80/tcp` protocol-suffixed inferred port produced syntactically valid labels that only failed later inside Traefik. Proxy init also accepted invalid ports/booleans that broke generated files and `status --json` far from the offending command. Fixes added numeric/range validation at config-generation and init time with targeted errors.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's generate logic already validates compose-file existence and service references and infers omitted ports, but never specifies validating that resolved/inferred port values are numeric and in range before emitting Traefik labels. Xcind proved skipping this pushes failures downstream into Traefik with opaque routing errors instead of a targeted generation-time error — a gap in Scind's otherwise fail-fast generation contract.
proposed_action: Add a port-value validation step to docs/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md 'Generation Logic' (resolved/inferred ports must be integers in 1–65535, protocol suffixes handled explicitly) and note in docs/specs/port-types.md that invalid ports fail at generation with an export-named error.

title: Networks declared external in overlays must have creation failures surfaced, not swallowed
what_xcind_learned: Xcind's workspace EXECUTE hook ran `docker network create ... >/dev/null 2>&1 || true`, suppressing ALL failures (daemon down, permission, invalid name) identically to the already-exists race. The generated overlay declares that same network `external: true`, so compose later failed with an opaque external-network error and no hint that Xcind tried and failed to create it. Fix: inspect-before-create, suppress only the exists race, and warn (naming the network + Docker error + that compose may fail) while staying non-fatal.
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's generated-override-files.md declares `dev-internal`/`scind-proxy` as `external: true`, and workspace-lifecycle.md startup steps 1–2 create the proxy/workspace network before `up`, but the spec is silent on failure handling. Xcind proved that when a dependency is declared external, a swallowed creation failure produces an opaque downstream compose error — a real design gap in the startup contract, not just a Bash bug.
proposed_action: In docs/specs/workspace-lifecycle.md 'Startup Sequence' (steps 1–2), specify that network/proxy creation failures must emit a diagnostic naming the resource and underlying error (because the overlay declares it external and compose will otherwise fail opaquely), and define whether the failure is fatal to `up`. Reference the external-network declaration in docs/specs/generated-override-files.md.

title: Apex implicit-primary should be scoped to PROXIED exports, so single-proxied apps get an apex with zero config
what_xcind_learned: Xcind gives an apex to the first PROXIED export, so an app with 1 proxied + N assigned exports has an unambiguous front door and gets an apex with zero annotation. Scind's implicit-primary rule counts ALL exports ('exactly one exported service' ⇒ implicitly primary), so `web`(proxied)+`db`(assigned) with nothing marked yields NO apex — even though apex hostnames only ever apply to proxied exports.
rationale: §2/§2a adversarial re-check outcome (the flagged apex case, SPLIT). This is the order-INDEPENDENT half of Xcind's positional evidence: scoping implicit-primary to proxied exports closes a genuine ergonomic hole without adopting bash-array positionalism, so it does NOT depend on ordered maps. A real, actionable learning that was about to be paved over by an ESCALATE/DIVERGENCE label. The residual 2+-proxied-none-marked case stays Scind-conservative (see L-0028).
proposed_action: In docs/specs/configuration-schemas.md (Primary Export Designation / 'Implicit primary', ~line 190) and docs/decisions/0013-apex-url-primary-designation.md (the 'Multiple exports, none marked primary → no apex' clause), refine implicit-primary from 'exactly one exported service' to 'exactly one PROXIED exported service ⇒ implicitly primary (apex-eligible); assigned exports do not count against implicit-primary eligibility.' Keep explicit `primary: true` required only when 2+ proxied exports compete.
adversarial_recheck: SPLIT from the flagged apex case. Adversarial re-check confirmed this default-policy refinement is order-independent and closes a real hole. The positional MECHANISM (L-0028) was subsequently resolved by HUMAN PRODUCT-CALL to a CANON-CHANGE (hybrid apex), so both apex halves now land as CANON-CHANGE and compose: L-0018 scopes apex-eligibility to proxied exports; L-0028 makes selection hybrid (explicit primary:true, else positional fallback).

title: SCIND_* service-discovery injection design is build-validated
what_xcind_learned: Building the discovery hook confirmed Scind's core `SCIND_{APP}_{EXPORT}_{SUFFIX}` injection design — env-safing rules (hyphens→underscores, uppercase), HTTPS-default base variables, protocol-specific `_HTTP_*`/`_HTTPS_*`, and apex variables on the primary/headlining export — is sound and implementable; Xcind ported it directly (as `XCIND_*`) reusing single-source helpers so env values stay byte-identical with proxy labels.
rationale: §2 directionality: Xcind's docs previously only promised these variables; the PoC actually produced them and validated the naming/scheme/apex rules end to end. Confirms an existing Scind decision rather than changing it. (The `_HOST`/`_HOST_PORT` coherence defect is captured separately in L-0003; this record confirms only the naming/scheme scaffold.)
proposed_action: No canon change. Annotate docs/specs/environment-variables.md that the injection variable schema is build-validated by the Xcind PoC. → P6 (low priority).

title: No application registry — applications derived from filesystem is validated
what_xcind_learned: Xcind maintains no application registry, per-app state store, manifest, or `remove` command. An application 'exists' iff a directory holds a `.xcind.sh` without the workspace marker; `xcind-application list` iterates the enclosing workspace's directory contents at call time; moving/copying/deleting an app is just moving/copying/deleting its directory. Xcind ran on this model end-to-end with no app-registry regressions.
rationale: §2 directionality: building the PoC validated the Scind design choice that applications need no separate registry and can be resolved from the workspace's directory contents. Confirms rather than challenges canon.
proposed_action: No canon change. Optionally note in Scind's application/workspace docs that the derived-not-tracked application model is PoC-validated. (Scind has no dedicated application-lifecycle spec; if one is written, this is the confirmed baseline.) → P6 (low priority).

title: Workspace state machine is descriptive; runtime inference is sufficient
what_xcind_learned: Xcind models NO explicit workspace state machine (no dormant/starting/running/error states, no state.yaml). It answers 'is the app running?' from Docker container status, 'which compose files?' from `.xcind.sh` at runtime, 'which hooks ran?' from the SHA cache, 'is the network present?' from Docker inspection — all on demand. Only persistent state is two narrow files (assigned-ports.tsv, workspaces.tsv registry).
rationale: §2 directionality: Scind's own state-management/workspace-lifecycle specs already say state is inferred from the environment at runtime. Xcind built exactly to that inference model and never needed the 6-state enumeration as a stored artifact — confirming Scind's state machine is descriptive documentation, not a required runtime structure.
proposed_action: No canon change. Keep Scind's workspace-lifecycle state table as descriptive prose; optionally add a one-line note that the PoC confirmed no state artifact is needed to drive lifecycle transitions. → P6 (low priority).

title: Xcind's push to out-stateless Scind failed — a workspace registry and assigned-port state are load-bearing
what_xcind_learned: Xcind repeatedly documented itself as stateless ('no state files, manifests, or registries'), but four source-review findings caught that claim as false: it maintains a workspace registry (workspaces.tsv) and assigned-port state (assigned-ports.tsv) under XDG_STATE_HOME, and `xcind-workspace list --prune` rewrites the registry. Docs were corrected to distinguish declarative config from narrow runtime state.
rationale: §2 directionality: Xcind attempted a leaner, more-stateless design than Scind and discovered it could not avoid a workspace registry and sticky assigned-port state — validating Scind's ADR-0005 structure/state separation and the state-management.md model as necessary, not over-engineered. The stale 'stateless' wording itself is Xcind self-drift (process-only for Xcind).
proposed_action: No Scind canon change — reinforces docs/specs/state-management.md and ADR-0005. Record as confirmation that a workspace registry + sticky assigned-port state are load-bearing. Log the 'stateless' wording correction as an Xcind-maintenance / P6 doc-hygiene item.

title: Config as sourceable .xcind.sh + XCIND_* env vars vs Scind's typed YAML
what_xcind_learned: Xcind expresses all workspace/app/proxy configuration as sourceable Bash (`.xcind.sh`) setting XCIND_* variables/arrays, with a three-level source chain. Scind uses typed YAML (workspace.yaml/application.yaml/proxy.yaml) and explicitly rejects environment-variable overrides for config values.
rationale: §2/§2a global-context Example A — settled implementation-shape divergence. Scind explicitly rejects env-var config; a compiled Go target gets parsed/validated YAML that is strictly safer than eval-ing shell. Not evidence Scind's design is wrong.
proposed_action: Registry line: 'Config model — Xcind uses sourceable .xcind.sh + XCIND_* env vars; Scind keeps typed YAML and rejects env-var config. Settled; do not adopt.'
rejected_canon_change_reasoning: Scind targets a compiled language where parsing/validating typed YAML is strictly better than sourcing/eval-ing an untrusted shell script (safety, schema validation, no code-execution surface). The bash config model is a PoC affordance of the Bash target, not proof Scind's YAML design is wrong. Scind's spec already deliberately rejects env-var config, so this is a considered position, not an oversight.

title: Environment-specific file selection via ${APP_ENV} expansion vs Scind flavors
what_xcind_learned: Xcind achieves environment-specific compose/env/config-file selection via shell variable expansion in file-pattern arrays (e.g. `compose.${APP_ENV}.yaml`, `.xcind.${APP_ENV}.sh`), with dual immediate/deferred expansion and automatic .override sibling derivation. Scind achieves the analogous goal with named `flavors` (declarative sets of compose_files) resolved via CLI/state.
rationale: §2: variable expansion in file patterns is an affordance of the sourceable-Bash config model (rides on L-0023's settled divergence). It does not prove Scind's typed `flavors` design wrong — flavors are a declarative, validated alternative to the same end.
proposed_action: Registry line: 'Environment-specific files — Xcind uses ${APP_ENV} expansion in bash file-pattern arrays; Scind uses typed named flavors resolved by CLI/state. Consequence of the config-model divergence; do not adopt into YAML canon.'
rejected_canon_change_reasoning: Variable expansion in file patterns is only meaningful because config is sourced Bash; in Scind's typed-YAML world the equivalent need is served by declarative flavors (validated, no eval). Xcind did not demonstrate flavors are inadequate — the two are alternative shapes for the same requirement, so this rides on the settled config-model divergence rather than being a canon defect.
adversarial_recheck: Not required — rides on Example A config-model divergence, low-risk.

title: Persisting config as a sourceable shell file creates a code-injection/escaping surface
what_xcind_learned: Xcind wrote proxy CLI flag values verbatim into a sourceable Bash `config.sh` inside double quotes; a value containing `"`, `$()`, backticks, backslashes, or newlines could corrupt the file or execute command substitution when the config was later sourced. Fix added an escaping helper plus temp-file+rename write.
rationale: §2 directionality: an intentional Bash-implementation compromise Scind should not adopt, and it is EARNED. The vulnerability exists only because Xcind persists configuration as an executable shell file that is re-sourced; the escaping burden and injection surface are artifacts of that mechanism.
proposed_action: Registry line: 'Xcind persists proxy config as a sourceable shell file (config.sh) and must shell-escape all persisted values; Scind persists structured YAML parsed by Viper and never executes config, so this escaping/injection concern does not transfer.'
rejected_canon_change_reasoning: Scind's configuration is structured YAML parsed (Viper), never sourced or executed. The value-escaping requirement and command-substitution risk are a direct consequence of Xcind's Bash choice to store config as sourceable shell; the failure class cannot occur in Scind, so adopting it into canon would be meaningless.

title: Own-app-only service-discovery scope is a PoC scope cut Scind should not adopt
what_xcind_learned: Scind injects every service's discovery variables into every container in the workspace (cross-app discovery). Xcind v1 injects only the current app's exports into its own containers — no workspace registry, no cross-app reads — because Xcind runs per-application. The variable shape is kept forward-compatible with a later workspace-wide v2.
rationale: §2/§2a adversarial re-check confirmed. Cross-app discovery is precisely Scind's value proposition; DNS network aliases (not env vars) are the real connectivity, so proxied cross-app vars are stable pure-name functions, and Scind's sticky global port state + generation-time resolution handle assigned-port cross-app reads without staleness. Xcind's cut is self-admittedly forced by its per-app execution model and explicitly forward-compatible with v2 — an implementation scope cut, not a discovered defect.
proposed_action: Registry line: 'Service-discovery scope — Xcind v1 injects only the current app's discovery vars into its own containers (per-application execution model, forward-compatible with a workspace-wide v2); Scind injects every service's vars into every container (cross-app discovery is core value, backed by DNS aliases + sticky global port state). Do not narrow Scind.'
rejected_canon_change_reasoning: Tested whether own-app scope reveals cross-app injection is unnecessary/harmful (coupling, topology leak, staleness). Fails: (a) DNS network aliases — not env vars — are the real connectivity, so proxied cross-app vars are stable pure-name functions; (b) Scind's sticky global port state + generation-time resolution handle assigned-port cross-app reads without staleness; (c) Xcind itself frames the cut as forced by its per-app model and explicitly forward-compatible with v2. Cross-app discovery remains Scind's coherent core value.
adversarial_recheck: PERFORMED (design-assumption candidate). Strongest canon-change argument (eager cross-app push is the broken assumption; discovery should be pull/DNS) FAILED — Scind already uses DNS aliases as the connectivity mechanism and neutralizes assigned-port staleness via sticky global state. Divergence stands.

title: Single sourceable .xcind.sh + marker vs Scind's two-file declarative workspace.yaml/application.yaml
what_xcind_learned: Xcind collapses Scind's two declarative YAML files into one sourced shell file: a directory is a workspace if `.xcind.sh` sets `XCIND_IS_WORKSPACE=1`, otherwise (with a `.xcind.sh`) an application; context detection is an upward walk to the first `.xcind.sh`, checking the parent for the marker. Exports are declared inline via `XCIND_PROXY_EXPORTS`; there is no separate app-owned service-contract file and no workspace applications-registry with path indirection.
rationale: §2/§2a adversarial re-check confirmed. The one-file framing is largely a mirage: Xcind still keeps a per-workspace AND a per-app config file in two dirs with two owners — only the filename/format was unified, a consequence of the settled shell-vs-YAML axis (L-0023). The load-bearing app-owned portable contract is PRESERVED by both. Additionally Scind's workspace.yaml applications-registry with explicit `path:` buys app-name→path indirection (apps as cloned repos/submodules at arbitrary paths) that Xcind's basename-of-child-dir convention forgoes — a real capability, not over-engineering.
proposed_action: Registry line: 'Config file model — Xcind uses one .xcind.sh format for both workspace and app roles (disambiguated by XCIND_IS_WORKSPACE=1), apps discovered by directory walk, exports declared inline; Scind keeps distinct workspace.yaml (with applications: registry + path: indirection) and app-owned application.yaml declarative contract. Both preserve the two-artifact + app-owned-portable-contract concept; format-unification is downstream of the settled shell-vs-YAML axis. No canon change.'
rejected_canon_change_reasoning: Tested whether Xcind proves the workspace/application split is unnecessary. Fails on two counts: (1) Xcind did not actually collapse the two artifacts — it retains a workspace-level and an app-level config file in separate dirs/owners; only filename/format was unified, which is the settled shell-vs-YAML consequence, not a design learning. (2) The app-owned-declarative-portable-contract property is real and preserved by BOTH systems, and Scind's workspace app-registry with path: provides name↔location indirection that Xcind's convention-over-configuration directory walk cannot express.
adversarial_recheck: PERFORMED (design-assumption candidate). Strongest canon-change argument (the workspace/app split is over-engineered; the distinction is one bit, not two document types) FAILED — Xcind retains two artifacts in two dirs and Scind's app-registry path-indirection is a real capability. Divergence stands (high confidence).

title: Apex primary-selection should be HYBRID: honor explicit primary:true, else fall back to positional (first-in-array)
what_xcind_learned: Xcind selects the apex/primary export positionally (first entry of the ordered `XCIND_PROXY_EXPORTS` bash array; user controls priority by ordering) with zero annotation. Scind mandates an explicit `primary: true` field and rejects positional selection. Xcind's positional-ONLY approach was partly a Bash-config limitation: there was no clean way to express a `primary` flag in the Bash config contract, so first-in-array was simply the easiest expression — not a considered rejection of explicit designation.
rationale: §2/§2a: HUMAN PRODUCT-CALL (overrides the adversarial re-check's provisional DIVERGENCE for the mechanism half). Classified CANON-CHANGE, not a plain divergence, because Xcind's positional-only was itself partly a Bash-config limitation (no clean way to express `primary` in the Bash contract; first-in-array was easiest) rather than a principled 'Scind should not adopt' choice — so the difference does not earn a divergence. The confirmed resolution: Scind should offer BOTH mechanisms (explicit wins, positional as an ergonomic fallback), pairing with the proxied-scoping in L-0018.
proposed_action: Amend docs/decisions/0013-apex-url-primary-designation.md to a HYBRID rule: an explicit `primary: true` export always wins; when NO export is marked primary, fall back to POSITIONAL selection (first-declared / first proxied export) and still emit an apex, instead of the current 'none marked → no apex'. Enabling the positional fallback requires Scind to define a stable declaration order for exports (switch `exported_services` from an unordered YAML map to a sequence, or adopt a documented first-declared rule) so 'first' is well-defined. Combine with L-0018 (apex-eligibility scoped to proxied exports).
adversarial_recheck: PERFORMED (the flagged apex case). Re-check provisionally SPLIT it: default-policy half → CANON-CHANGE (L-0018); mechanism half → DIVERGENCE (unordered-YAML-map principle). HUMAN PRODUCT-CALL then OVERRODE the mechanism verdict to CANON-CHANGE (hybrid), noting Xcind's positional-only was partly a Bash-config limitation, not a principled Scind rejection. Divergence label withdrawn.

title: Read-only invocation paths must resolve config without writing shared state; user-facing config keys need a deprecation path
what_xcind_learned: Two operational conveniences: (a) `XCIND_NO_REGISTRY` suppresses the workspace-registry write during discovery so read-only callers (the prompt helper) don't mutate shared state while still resolving all XCIND_* variables; (b) a back-compat shim auto-migrates the deprecated `XCIND_ENV_FILES` to `XCIND_COMPOSE_ENV_FILES` with a warning and a precedence rule when both are set.
rationale: §2: neither is a design position. XCIND_NO_REGISTRY is a Bash-side guard for a read-only invocation path (state-write suppression); the XCIND_ENV_FILES shim is a migration/deprecation mechanic. The substantive design lesson under the rename (the two env-file scopes must be named distinctly) is captured in L-0014; what remains here is process.
proposed_action: No canon change. Note for Scind's own build (P6): any read-only inspection path (list/prompt/status) should resolve config without writing the workspace registry; renames of user-facing config keys warrant a deprecation/migration path.

title: Generator subcommands should have uniform stdout/file semantics and be named for their output, not a presumed consumer
what_xcind_learned: Xcind's `xcind-config` had a special-case `--generate-ide-configuration=DIR` (required directory, hardcoded filename, no stdout) inconsistent with its other `--generate-*` flags. It was unified to `--generate-docker-compose-configuration[=FILE]` (stdout or file) and renamed because the output (a fully-resolved compose config) is useful in devcontainers, CI, debugging, and scripting — not just IDEs.
rationale: §2: a lesson about CLI interface consistency on Xcind's `xcind-config` binary, which has no Scind analog (Scind uses `scind workspace generate` producing `.generated/*.override.yaml` files). It changes how a generator is invoked/named, not what the product must promise, so it does not reach Scind's design.
proposed_action: No canon change. Optional CLI-ergonomics guideline for Scind's build: generator subcommands should support uniform stdout/file output and be named for their output artifact, not a presumed consumer. → P6.

title: Two-track documentation (user Diátaxis + engineering LDS) once a real tool exists
what_xcind_learned: LDS serves contributors and AI agents but is the wrong shape for end users who just want to install, configure `.xcind.sh`, and run. Once the README hit ~760 lines acting as a stand-in manual, Xcind split into a user-facing Diátaxis track (`docs/`) and an engineering LDS track (`engineering/`), with intentional narrative-only duplication of reference material and single-source-of-truth kept within each track.
rationale: §2: a lesson about how to structure documentation for two audiences, not about product design or any Scind guarantee. Scind is currently engineering-documentation-only (LDS); when built 'for real' it will need an end-user track too — a relevant process lesson, not a canon-change to any design decision or spec.
proposed_action: No canon change. Flag for Scind's documentation process (P6): when Scind ships as a usable tool, add a user-facing Diátaxis track alongside the LDS engineering canon, following Xcind's cross-track linking and narrative-only-duplication conventions.

title: CLI argument robustness: missing flag values, surplus/duplicate positionals, explicit-vs-auto failure semantics
what_xcind_learned: Multiple entrypoints read `$2` for value flags under `set -u` (so `init --name` aborted with an unbound-variable diagnostic instead of a usage error), silently ignored unexpected trailing args (`up --force extra`, `up --bogus` degrading to a plain up), overwrote repeated positional DIR args, and let explicit `xcind-proxy up` exit 0 even when Traefik failed. Fixes added missing-value guards, strict unexpected-arg rejection, and a strict-mode flag so explicit CLI startup propagates failure while the auto-start EXECUTE hook stays non-fatal.
rationale: §2: CLI input-handling and error-propagation quality issues, not product-design gaps. Scind already specifies strict failure for explicit user commands and has no auto-start-from-hook dual path, so the strict/non-fatal tension is a Xcind-architecture artifact; the unbound-variable/`set -u` modes are Bash-specific.
proposed_action: No canon change; lands in Xcind maintenance / P6. The general principles (explicit commands fail loudly; reject unknown/surplus args; validate value flags) could weakly inform docs/reference/appendices/cli/error-messages.md but need no canon change since Scind's CLI is structured (Cobra) and already fails hard on explicit ops.

title: Pervasive eng-doc-to-code drift → the sync effort needs a periodic source-review sweep
what_xcind_learned: Across all four source-review areas the bulk of findings were documentation drift: specs claiming statelessness, cache-key summaries omitting real inputs (env files, additional configs, XCIND_TOOLS, host-gateway detected value), context-labels attributed to the wrong hook, apex/TLS behavior files describing pre-TLS output, and project-layout trees missing current entrypoints/libraries. All were code-correct/docs-stale, closed by editing Xcind's own docs.
rationale: §2: every one is Xcind's implementation-layer docs lagging Xcind's code (authority uniformly 'code correct, docs stale'). None reveal a design property Scind's canon lacks; they teach a maintenance-cadence lesson about keeping the lower LDS layers synced to code — the P1 trust prerequisite in action.
proposed_action: No Scind canon impact. Feed the P6 maintenance procedure: recommend a periodic source-review/doc-drift sweep (the cadence that produced these ledgers) and record the recurring high-drift areas (cache-key input lists, hook label ownership, project-layout trees, stateless wording) as standing checklist items.

title: Per-export single `tls` key vs Scind's protocol+visibility dual model; visibility labels are Scind-ahead
what_xcind_learned: Scind models a proxied export as one or more `ports:` entries each with explicit `protocol: http|https` plus a `visibility: public|protected|private` label. Xcind collapsed this to one proxied export carrying a single `tls` key (`auto`/`require`/`disable`, globally gated by `XCIND_PROXY_TLS_MODE`), emitting two parallel routers `{...}-http`/`{...}-https`, and DROPPED Scind's `visibility` concept entirely (emits no visibility labels).
rationale: §2 cannot be cleanly applied — the reshape mixes two opposite-flowing differences. (a) CANON-CHANGE reading: requiring separate per-protocol port entries plus a `require`-redirect is more complex than one ergonomic `tls` key (overlaps the redirect-mechanism gap in L-0008). (b) SCIND-AHEAD/P5 reading: `visibility` (public/protected/private) is a documented external-tooling access-control contract Xcind chose NOT to exercise — dropping it is a Scind→Xcind gap (P5 material), not proof Scind is wrong, and cannot be filed as a P3 divergence. Because the two facets flow opposite directions, route to a human/P6 call rather than default to any single label.
proposed_action: ESCALATE. (a) Consider documenting an ergonomic single-key TLS mode in docs/specs/port-types.md as an alternative to per-protocol port entries (fold with L-0008). (b) Route the visibility-label drop to P5 as a Scind-ahead capability ('specified in Scind, not exercised in Xcind') — NOT a P3 learning and NOT a divergence.

title: Workspace-wide generated manifest: necessary orchestrator view, or redundant with on-demand --json?
what_xcind_learned: Scind computes a workspace-wide read-only `.generated/manifest.yaml` (aggregated hostnames/aliases/env vars for discoverability, tool integration, debugging, cache-comparison). Xcind ships NO manifest; it serves the same needs per-app and on-demand via `xcind-config --json` (SHA-cached `config.json` written after hooks). There is no aggregated workspace-level computed view.
rationale: §2 cannot be cleanly applied and must not default to DIVERGENCE. Reading A (manifest unnecessary): Xcind delivers discoverability/tooling/debug entirely from per-app `--json`, suggesting Scind's aggregated manifest is redundant with an on-demand command. Reading B (keep it): Scind is a workspace ORCHESTRATOR (`workspace up` drives all apps at once), whereas Xcind is a per-app tool, so a single aggregated topology view has value Xcind's architecture never needed to produce — its absence is not a clean disproof. A genuine design-assumption call for P6.
proposed_action: ESCALATE to P6 reconciliation: decide whether Scind's manifest.yaml should be (a) retained as the orchestrator's aggregated view, (b) demoted to a derivable `--json` command output, or (c) kept but explicitly defined as cache-derived (with the L-0006 freshness contract). Do not silently file as divergence.

title: Should Scind adopt a named, extensible generation hook pipeline (CONFIGURED/RESOLVED/GENERATE/EXECUTE)?
what_xcind_learned: Xcind decomposed Scind's monolithic `workspace generate` into a named pipeline of phases, each backed by a hook array, with a load-bearing rule: GENERATE hooks must be PURE (no runtime side effects; may be skipped on cache hit) while runtime side effects (create network, start proxy, write host-env file) belong in EXECUTE, which always runs. Custom hooks can be registered in `.xcind.sh`, making generation extensible.
rationale: §2 cannot be cleanly applied. Largely additive (Xcind-ahead), not a disproof: Scind already separates `workspace generate` (pure-ish) from `workspace up` (runtime), so the pure-vs-runtime boundary is not absent — Xcind refined it to per-hook granularity and added extensibility Scind lacks. Not CANON-CHANGE (the purity gap that IS a real Scind defect is captured in L-0005), not DIVERGENCE (Scind would plausibly WANT an extensible pipeline), not CANON-CONFIRM. Flag for P4 forward-port consideration.
proposed_action: ESCALATE to P4/forward-port: evaluate whether Scind should adopt a named, extensible generation pipeline with a documented pure-generator vs runtime-precondition contract. Not a canon defect; a candidate feature Scind pulls forward if it wants third-party generation hooks.

title: App-side self-declared workspace membership inverts Scind's workspace-owns-applications model
what_xcind_learned: In Xcind an application can join a workspace WITHOUT being nested under a workspace root: setting `XCIND_WORKSPACE="name"` in its own `.xcind.sh` triggers late-bind, flipping the run into workspace mode (network + proxy routing) with `XCIND_WORKSPACE_ROOT` derived from `XCIND_APP_ROOT`. Late-bind is skipped if already in a detected workspace (discovered value wins) or if `XCIND_WORKSPACE` is unset. This inverts Scind's model, where the workspace (workspace.yaml) OWNS and enumerates its applications by path and apps live inside the workspace tree.
rationale: §2 cannot be cleanly applied and must not default to DIVERGENCE. Xcind demonstrated a genuinely different ownership direction (app-declares-membership) enabling an app in its own repo elsewhere to join a workspace without being cloned into the tree. Whether this is a CANON-CHANGE (Scind's rigid nesting is limiting) or a DIVERGENCE (Scind deliberately wants the workspace to own the app roster for orchestration) is a real design-assumption call — Scind's `path:` field hints at flexibility but its directory-structure clones apps into the workspace. Burden favors CANON-CHANGE, but the ownership inversion is significant enough to warrant a P6 judgment.
proposed_action: ESCALATE to P6: decide whether Scind should support app-side self-declared workspace membership (decoupled from directory nesting) or intentionally keep workspace-owns-applications enumeration. Record that Xcind proved the inverted binding workable in practice.

title: Scind's *_HOST_GATEWAY container env-var mandate is UNMET in Xcind (extra_hosts only)
what_xcind_learned: Scind's host-gateway-resolution spec says the resolved workstation-host value SHOULD also be exposed as an environment variable inside containers (`SCIND_HOST_GATEWAY`), with Xdebug `client_host` as the motivating use case. Xcind's as-built `xcind-host-gateway-hook` only writes `extra_hosts: host.docker.internal:<value>` per service (honoring `XCIND_HOST_GATEWAY_ENABLED=0` and the `XCIND_HOST_GATEWAY` override) — it does NOT inject any `environment:` var. The detection algorithm and SHA-inclusion are implemented; the env-exposure SHOULD is not.
rationale: §2 cannot be cleanly applied: not proof Scind's design is wrong (so not CANON-CHANGE) and not an earned intentional compromise (so not DIVERGENCE) — an as-built GAP where Scind's SHOULD-level mandate is untested/unimplemented in the PoC. Sits on the P5 as-built boundary; escalating per the plan's explicit instruction to flag it. (Confirmed both directions: Scind DOES mandate the env var; Xcind does NOT implement it.)
proposed_action: ESCALATE / route to P5: Scind's `*_HOST_GATEWAY` container env exposure (host-gateway-resolution.md 'Environment Variable Exposure') is unimplemented in Xcind, which only emits `extra_hosts`. Either implement env injection in xcind-host-gateway-lib.bash to validate the mandate, or downgrade the Scind spec's SHOULD if extra_hosts-only proves sufficient. No canon change proposed until Xcind exercises the env path.

title: Flavors were left out of Xcind by design — the mechanism is coupled to Scind's persistent state model
what_xcind_learned: Scind expresses environment-specific configuration via named declarative `flavors` (sets of compose_files) whose ACTIVE selection is recorded in persistent state (state.yaml) and resolved via CLI. Xcind dropped flavors entirely, using stateless `${APP_ENV}` variable expansion in file-pattern arrays instead. WHY they were dropped: Xcind is deliberately stateless and per-app with no state store to persist an 'active flavor', so a flavor mechanism (which needs somewhere to record which flavor is active) had nothing to hang on; env-var-driven file selection achieves the same environment-switching outcome without any persistent state.
rationale: §2 cannot be cleanly applied and must not default to DIVERGENCE (surfaced as a learning candidate per HUMAN PRODUCT-CALL). Reading A (learning): Xcind proved environment-specific config is achievable WITHOUT a stateful flavor mechanism, suggesting Scind's flavors — which couple to state.yaml's active-flavor record — may be heavier than the outcome requires. Reading B (keep flavors): flavors are declarative, validated, and discoverable in a way stateless env-var expansion is not, and Scind's orchestrator model persists an active flavor deliberately (so `workspace up` without args reuses the last flavor). The datapoint the human call asked to surface is the WHY: flavor selection is coupled to persistent state, which Xcind's stateless design has none of — so its absence in Xcind is architecture-forced, not a proof flavors are wrong. Genuine design-assumption call.
proposed_action: ESCALATE to P6/P5: decide whether Scind retains stateful named flavors or additionally offers a stateless env-var-driven alternative. Record the WHY (flavor selection is coupled to persistent active-flavor state in state.yaml; Xcind is stateless so used `${APP_ENV}` file-pattern expansion instead). Cross-ref L-0024 (the config-model consequence) and L-0022 (Xcind's statelessness limits). Feeds docs/specs/state-management.md + docs/specs/configuration-schemas.md flavor-resolution review.
